macro_segments.title: Supervision of Jewish Wine: The Crack in the Door and Line of Sight Principle

micro_segments.title: The house with locked door and wine: Rava's ruling on barrel proximity to crack
Explanation of the principle: visibility and knowledge of supervision

macro_segments.title: Jewish and Non-Jewish Housemates: Shared Dwelling Supervision

micro_segments.title: Wine in lower story with Jew above: Normal supervision by presence
Interruption by fighting: Non-Jew locks door and returns alone
Rava's ruling: Non-Jew fears Jew will return, therefore wine is permitted

macro_segments.title: The Inn Case and the Principle of Nitpas Alav K'Ganav (Fear of Consequences)

micro_segments.title: Non-Jew found among barrels in inn: Rava's conditional ruling
If discovered handling wine would bring consequences, wine is permitted
If discovered without consequences, wine is forbidden

macro_segments.title: Distinguishing Nitpas K'Ganav from Aishtemutei (Excuse vs. Consequences)

micro_segments.title: House with non-Jew found among barrels: Rava's ruling on excuses
If non-Jew has excuse for being there, wine is forbidden
If no excuse for being there, wine is permitted
Rashi's explanation: Excuse means leisure to linger and handle wine
Distinction between having right to be somewhere (nistech alav k'ganav) versus having no right (aishtemutei)

macro_segments.title: Cases of Jewish and Non-Jewish Companions Leaving Wine Unattended

micro_segments.title: Non-Jew assumes Jew will return from Mincha to check on wine
Jew and non-Jew drinking together; Jew leaves for Mincha service
Jew and non-Jew on boat; Jew leaves for Shabbat preparation
Issur Giyora's perception: Jews will break Shabbat to protect money
Explanation of Jewish leniency regarding Shabbat and wallets on marketplace

macro_segments.title: Thieves and Troops Among Wine Barrels: Double Doubt Principle (Safek Bi'ah)

micro_segments.title: Lion in winepress; non-Jew hides among barrels for protection
Non-Jew fears Jew will also hide there and watch him
Thieves break open many barrels of wine in Pumbedita
Rava: Most thieves are Jewish, not non-Jewish; wine is permitted
Case in Nehardea: Shmuel permits wine even when non-Jewish thieves opened barrels
Double doubt principle: Maybe thief was Jewish, or if non-Jewish, looking for money not wine

macro_segments.title: Young Girl with Wine Froth and Generosity in Presumptions

micro_segments.title: Non-Jewish girl found with wine froth on her hand among barrels
Rava: Assume froth came from outside barrel, not from inside handling
Young girl: Not aware of avodah zarah context, so handling permitted

macro_segments.title: Thieves and Troops Among Wine Barrels: Double Doubt Principle (Safek Bi'ah) (continued)

micro_segments.title: Troops open multiple barrels seeking cash, not wine; wine is permitted

macro_segments.title: Comparison of Tumah V'Taharah and Yayin Nesech Concerns

micro_segments.title: Female wine seller gives key to non-Jew: Job is to guard key, not access cabinet
Abaye: Parallel in Tahara law where non-Jew watches keys to tahur fruit
Principle: Concern for Tumah V'Taharah is weightier than for Yayin Nesech
Rav: Adjacent yards divided by hedge; Am Ha'aretz boundary concerns Tumah but not wine
Rabbi Yochanan: Boundary line sufficient for both Tumah and wine concerns
Cases of rooftop fruit: Reaching hand over creates excuse concerns
Debate of Tanna'im: Whether reaching hand constitutes sufficient excuse

macro_segments.title: Troops in Times of Peace Versus War: Nisuch Context

micro_segments.title: Troops in peacetime: Open barrels forbidden, closed barrels permitted
Troops in war: Both open and closed barrels permitted—no leisure for nisuch
Rav Mari's distinction: Too distracted for nisuch, but time for other acts

topical_tags.term: Nitpas Alav K'Ganav (Fear of consequences if caught)
Aishtemutei/Aiet Lei Lishtamutei (Has excuse for being somewhere)
Line of sight and visibility of supervision
Safek Bi'ah Tahor (Double doubt if entry occurred)
Safek Maga (Doubt if object was touched)
Rava (Authority ruling on multiple cases)
Rashi (Commentary and explanations)
Sherut Hamiftach (Guarding keys without access rights)
Tumah V'Taharah (Purity and impurity laws parallels)
Am Ha'aretz (Non-scholar, non-careful Jew)
Ganav/Ganavim (Thief/Thieves)
Mechoza (Place of residence with Jewish majority)
Nesachim/Nisuch (Libations to idols)
Tzorchei Rabbim (Communal needs/emergency)
Shabbat and Jewish observance
Issur Giyora (Statement of non-Jew about Jews and Shabbat)
Rav (Authority and dispute partner with Rabbi Yochanan)
Rabbi Yochanan (Authority in dispute with Rav)
Rabban Shimon ben Gamliel (Stringent position on reachability)
Shmuel (Authority permitting wine in Nehardea case)
Boleshet/Troops (Military forces confiscating supplies)
Pesach/Passover (Time references in some cases)
